title: stainful: From OpenAPI Spec to Idiomatic Python SDK
description: Walk the stainful pipeline — an OpenAPI spec and a Stainless config are loaded, resolved into a language-agnostic intermediate representation, then emitted as a Python SDK that wraps a hand-written vendored runtime.
prerequisites: Skim the project readme for the quick pitch

steps.title: What is stainful?
steps.description: stainful turns an OpenAPI spec and a Stainless config into a fully-typed, idiomatic Python SDK — pagination, retries, streaming, sync and async — no hosted service. This is the whole map, colored by file type. The next steps zoom into each piece of the pipeline: the loaders that read the inputs, the intermediate representation in the middle, the emitter that writes Python, and the runtime that gets vendored into every generated SDK.

steps.title: The command-line entrypoint
steps.description: Everything starts at the command line. The entrypoint wires the four pipeline stages together: load the config, load the spec, build the intermediate representation, then emit. Read this part first — it is the shortest possible summary of the entire architecture.

steps.title: Stage one — load the Stainless config
steps.description: The first stage parses the Stainless config using a position-aware YAML library so it can attach precise line and column diagnostics. One file defines the typed shape of the config; the other turns the raw document into that shape with quotable error messages.

steps.title: Stage two — load and resolve the OpenAPI spec
steps.description: The second stage reads the OpenAPI document and then resolves it. The resolver walks every reference without infinite-looping on cycles, and it merges inherited schemas so downstream code sees a single flattened shape. This is the layer that absorbs all the messiness of real-world OpenAPI.

steps.title: Stage three — the intermediate representation
steps.description: The intermediate representation is the heart of stainful — a fully-resolved, language-agnostic model of the SDK. Polymorphic schemas become real tagged unions, optionality stays three-valued so required, optional, and nullable remain distinct, and resources are shaped around the domain rather than the raw endpoint list.

steps.title: Stage four — emit the Python SDK
steps.description: The emitter is a thin templating renderer that walks the intermediate representation and writes a Python package. A small companion handles Python naming conventions — snake-cased methods, capitalized types, singularized resource prefixes. Keeping the emitter deliberately dumb is the point: idiomatic behavior lives in the runtime, not in per-endpoint templates.

steps.title: The vendored runtime
steps.description: This area does not run inside the generator — it is copied verbatim into every generated SDK. Inside it: the sync and async HTTP client, the auto-pagination iterator, the typed server-sent-event streaming layer, and the typed-error hierarchy. Because the runtime is hand-written and audited once, the emitter can stay simple and every generated SDK inherits the same battle-tested behavior.

steps.title: Conformance — the project dogfoods itself
steps.description: The project dogfoods itself: a real production config and spec live alongside their checked-in generated SDK, and continuous integration fails if regenerating changes even a single byte. Fixture-driven tests cover pagination, multipart uploads, binary responses, and streaming end to end.

steps.title: The whole pipeline, in one view
steps.description: Zoom back out: the loaders feed the intermediate representation, which feeds the emitter, which writes a package that imports the vendored runtime. A good first contribution is to pick a failing fixture and trace it through the stages you just walked.
